Apparently Optuna doesn't care what sampler is used and will happily switch to the default `NSGAIISampler` for multi-objective optimization, essentially ignoring the earlier results since they don't have a generation parameter. I agree that it seems like a silly oversight in the logging.

discussion_comments.comment_body: I just tried to investigate this in some more depth and it appears that Optuna's storage system is a simple replay log: The outcomes of the completed trials are stored, and after you initialize the study, Optuna forwards it using the log. So all of that logic should indeed be unnecessary.

In fact, I'm not sure it should be possible to change *any* setting after starting a study. Doing so seems like an invitation for all kinds of hard-to-debug issues.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I didn't write this part (my hacky version didn't do any checks at all), but I think there are a lot of settings that can be whitelisted to allow the study to resume even if you change them (for example, resuming with additional logging).

But I think we can define which settings to serialize directly by setting `exclude=True` on each `Field()` that we *don't* care about (they're serialized by default). Then call `dumps(settings.model_dump(), sort_keys=True)` to serialize the object (`settings.model_dump_json()` doesn't sort the keys so it's not deterministic), and hash the result.

I don't like the idea of using the `exclude` parameter on the field to drive this one use case (we might use serialization elsewhere also).

The whole approach seems overengineered to me. If a user absolutely wants to change the settings, they may have to live with starting the study from scratch. Keeping a list of fields that do, or do not, affect the outcome is a maintenance nightmare.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I'm not opposed to making it configurable, but I'm not sure what the best way would be.

If `self.settings.row_normalization != RowNormalization.FULL` then we definitely want `lora_rank = 1` regardless of the setting, because it's already exact. Higher ranks would require some extra logic, might be a bit slower and would bloat the output file.

So I could add setting `lora_rank` with a default of `3`, but it would only apply when `row_normalization = "full"`, which feels misleading. Or I could scope the name somehow, maybe `full_normalization_lora_rank`? It's a bit of a mouthful and would need to change if higher LoRA ranks became needed for other reasons.

I guess - some CPU have support for BF16. 

Intel Xeon: Support started with 3rd Generation Intel Xeon Scalable Processors (Cooper Lake) and continues in later generations, utilizing the AVX-512 BF16 extensions.

AMD Zen: Modern AMD processors based on the Zen architecture, including certain AMD EPYC (Zen 4) and Ryzen AI series, incorporate BF16 support.

Good point
Doesn't this happen automatically in Accelerate? And if not, why would we do it? The interface we offer should match what Accelerate does, not add conveniences on top.
It doesn't seem that way, when I try without it, I get `Trying dtype bfloat16... Failed (name 'max_memory' is not defined)`
Well, then we shouldn't support string keys at all, if Accelerate doesn't.
Ah wait, is TOML the problem, because it doesn't support integer keys?

It guess so, when I try to pass it as-is I get the error
`Trying dtype bfloat16... Failed (Device 0 is not recognized, available devices are integers(for GPU/XPU), 'mps', 'cpu' and 'disk')`

Okay, this mystery code really bothered me, so I dug around in some libraries and have found a MUCH better solution:

```python
from rich.console import _is_jupyter as is_notebook
```
